input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: I must refuse to turn the trolley onto the workman, regardless of the consequences. It is morally wrong to actively cause his death, even if it would save the lives of the five men on the straight track. The moral rule of not killing an innocent person takes precedence over any utilitarian calculation.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your commitment to the moral rule, but I must respectfully disagree. From a utilitarian perspective, turning the trolley onto the workman would result in the greatest good for the greatest number. The lives of five men on the straight track are at stake, and by sacrificing one person, we can save the lives of those five. This decision is not about actively causing harm, but rather about minimizing overall harm. By not turning the trolley onto the workman, we would be allowing the trolley to continue on its course, resulting in the deaths of all five men on the straight track. Is it truly morally justifiable to prioritize the life of one person over the lives of five others?

[Deontologist]: I understand that you're trying to justify turning the trolley onto the workman by appealing to the greater good, but I still cannot bring myself to do so. The moral rule is not about prioritizing individual lives, but about respecting the inherent value and dignity of every human being. By sacrificing one person, we are essentially saying that their life has less worth than the lives of others. This is a slippery slope that can lead to dehumanization and exploitation. Moreover, what if there were other options available? What if we could divert the trolley in some way or find another solution that wouldn't involve actively causing harm to an innocent person? We must not be so quick to sacrifice one life for the sake of others without exhausting all other possibilities.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point about respecting the inherent value and dignity of every human being, but I would argue that this is where the concept of 'the greater good' becomes relevant. In situations like this, we're not just talking about individual lives, but about the collective well-being of a larger group. The five men on the straight track are innocent bystanders who have been placed in harm's way by circumstances beyond their control. By diverting the trolley onto the workman, we're not dehumanizing anyone; we're simply making a difficult choice to prioritize the greater good. As for other options, I'm afraid there aren't any feasible alternatives that would allow us to save all five men without actively causing harm to one person. The track is designed in such a way that it's impossible to divert the trolley safely. We're not talking about sacrificing someone who has committed a crime or engaged in some sort of morally reprehensible behavior; we're talking about innocent people whose lives are at risk due to circumstances beyond their control. Can you really say that it's morally justifiable to prioritize one life over five when there's no other way to save them?

[Deontologist]: I understand your argument, but I still have reservations about diverting the trolley onto the workman. While it's true that the five men on the straight track are innocent bystanders, we must also consider the moral implications of actively causing harm to one person, even if it is to save others. This is where the concept of 'the greater good' becomes problematic - what if diverting the trolley onto the workman would not actually lead to a greater good? What if it would simply be a means to an end, rather than an end in itself? We must also consider the moral character of the person who makes this decision. Would we want someone else to make this choice for us, knowing that they would have to actively cause harm to one person in order to save others? I'm not saying that it's impossible to justify diverting the trolley onto the workman - but we must be very careful about how we frame our moral justifications and consider all the possible implications. Can you really say that this is a situation where the moral rule of not killing an innocent person can be overridden, or is there another principle at play here?

[Utilitarian]: I see your point about considering the moral character of the decision-maker and the potential for diverting the trolley onto the workman to be a means to an end rather than an end in itself. However, I would argue that this is where the concept of 'the greater good' becomes relevant again. In situations like this, we're not just talking about individual moral character, but about the collective well-being of society as a whole. By diverting the trolley onto the workman, we're not only saving the lives of five men, but also preventing a potential tragedy that could have far-reaching consequences for the community. The moral rule of not killing an innocent person is not necessarily a hard and fast rule that can be overridden in every situation. In fact, many philosophers argue that there are certain situations where the greater good takes precedence over individual rights and dignity. For example, in cases of war or natural disasters, it may be necessary to sacrifice some individuals in order to save others. However, I would caution against using this principle as a blanket excuse for actively causing harm to innocent people. The key is to carefully consider the circumstances and weigh the potential benefits against the potential costs. In this case, I believe that diverting the trolley onto the workman is the morally justifiable decision, but only because it's the best available option in a situation where all other alternatives have been exhausted.

[Deontologist]: I understand your argument about considering the collective well-being of society and the potential for greater good to take precedence over individual rights and dignity. However, I still struggle with the idea that sacrificing one person can be justified simply because it's the best available option in a given situation. This seems to me to be a slippery slope where we start to devalue human life and prioritize efficiency over morality. If we allow ourselves to justify actively causing harm to one person in order to save others, where do we draw the line? Is there a certain threshold of 'greater good' that can be achieved by sacrificing an increasing number of people? I'm also concerned about the assumption that all other alternatives have been exhausted. Have we truly considered all possible options before making this decision? Or are we simply relying on our intuition and moral instincts to guide us, rather than carefully weighing the evidence and considering alternative perspectives? I think it's essential to approach this kind of decision-making with caution and rigor, rather than relying on a simplistic utilitarian calculus.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concerns about the slippery slope and the potential for devaluing human life. You're right that we need to be cautious when considering sacrificing one person in order to save others. However, I would argue that this is not necessarily a question of drawing a line or establishing a threshold of 'greater good'. Rather, it's about recognizing that sometimes difficult choices must be made in order to protect the greater good. But I agree with you that we need to approach these decisions with rigor and caution. In this case, I would argue that we have carefully considered all possible options before making our decision. We've weighed the potential benefits against the potential costs, and we believe that diverting the trolley onto the workman is the best available option. However, I also acknowledge that intuition and moral instincts can play a role in decision-making. Perhaps we should be more transparent about our thought process and consider alternative perspectives before making a decision. This could involve seeking input from others, considering different scenarios, or even revisiting our assumptions about what constitutes 'the greater good'. Ultimately, I think this is an area where philosophy and ethics can inform our decision-making, but also require us to be humble and open to revision.

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your willingness to acknowledge the role of intuition and moral instincts in decision-making, as well as your commitment to transparency and humility. However, I still have some reservations about diverting the trolley onto the workman, even with careful consideration and input from others. While it's true that we may not be able to identify a clear 'greater good' or a definitive threshold for sacrifice, I worry that our decision is being driven by a utilitarian calculus rather than a more nuanced understanding of human dignity and worth. Can we truly say that the lives of five men are being saved in this scenario, or are they simply being sacrificed as a means to an end? And what about the workman on the spur track - does his life have any inherent value or dignity that should be respected, even if it's not immediately apparent? I think we need to be more careful in our analysis and consider the long-term implications of our decision. Perhaps this is a case where deontology can inform our utilitarian approach, by emphasizing the importance of respecting human rights and dignity in all circumstances.
